name: Blackberry
description: One of the most recognizable and abundant wild fruits in North America and Europe. Grows on thorny arching canes. Ripe berries are deep black (not red), glossy, and sweet-tart.

identification.leaves: Compound with 3–5 toothed leaflets, green above, pale and slightly hairy beneath
identification.stem: Arching canes with sharp curved thorns; green to reddish-purple
identification.fruit: Aggregate berry, green → red → deep glossy black when ripe; hollow when picked (unlike raspberry)
identification.lookalikes: Raspberry (similar but fruit is hollow and usually red/golden). Pokeweed berries (toxic — grow on thick purple stems with smooth oval leaves on a single tall plant, berries in elongated clusters)

lookalikeDanger: Low — mainly confused with raspberries (edible) or occasionally pokeweed (toxic). Always confirm thorny canes and aggregate berry structure.
harvestNotes: Pick only fully ripe (dead black) berries. Wear long sleeves and gloves. Berries don't store long — refrigerate or freeze immediately.
nutritionHighlights: Vitamin C, Vitamin K, fiber, manganese, antioxidants
preparationMethods: Eat fresh, jams, pies, wine, infused vinegar, dried
medicinalUses: Leaf tea for diarrhea and sore throats; antioxidant-rich
ecologicalRole: Critical wildlife food source. Dense thickets provide nesting habitat for birds and small mammals.
safetyWarnings: Avoid berries near roads sprayed with herbicides
Wash thoroughly before eating
Don't harvest in areas treated with pesticides

name: Elderberry
edibility: Good when cooked — RAW BERRIES ARE MILDLY TOXIC
description: Fast-growing native shrub producing large flat-topped clusters (umbels) of tiny dark berries. Highly valued for immune-supporting elderberry syrup and elderflower cordial. IMPORTANT: raw berries, leaves, bark, and unripe berries contain cyanogenic glycosides — always cook berries before consuming.

identification.leaves: Opposite, pinnately compound with 5–9 toothed leaflets; strong unpleasant smell when crushed
identification.stem: Woody shrub/small tree with pithy stems; grayish-brown bark with prominent lenticels
identification.flower: Large flat-topped clusters (15–25 cm) of tiny, creamy-white 5-petaled flowers with sweet musky scent
identification.fruit: Tiny (4–6mm) round berries, deep purple-black in large hanging clusters
identification.lookalikes: Pokeweed (toxic — thick purple stems, large smooth leaves, elongated grape-like berry clusters). Water hemlock (deadly — white umbrella flowers, not berry clusters)

lookalikeDanger: MODERATE — pokeweed and water hemlock can occur in similar habitats. Confirm compound leaves with unpleasant smell, pithy stems, and flat-topped berry clusters.
harvestNotes: Harvest entire clusters when fully dark (not mixed red/black). Cut cluster stems — don't strip individual berries in the field. Process within 24 hours.
nutritionHighlights: Vitamin C, anthocyanins, flavonoids, quercetin, zinc
preparationMethods: Elderberry syrup (always cook), jelly, wine, elderflower cordial (flowers), fritters
medicinalUses: Antiviral, immune stimulation, flu symptom relief, anti-inflammatory
ecologicalRole: Major wildlife food source. Flowers support pollinators. Provides dense nesting cover.
safetyWarnings: ALWAYS cook berries — raw berries cause nausea/vomiting
Avoid leaves and bark
Do not eat unripe (red) berries
Consult doctor if pregnant

name: Wood Sorrel
description: A delightful, clover-like plant with three heart-shaped leaflets and small white or yellow flowers. The leaves have a refreshing lemon-sour taste from oxalic acid — excellent in salads, as a trail snack, or as a garnish.

identification.leaves: Three heart-shaped leaflets that fold down at night; bright green, often with purple markings beneath
identification.stem: Thin, delicate, stemmed from a central rosette
identification.flower: Small, 5-petaled, white with pink veins or yellow depending on species
identification.fruit: Small explosive seed capsules when ripe
identification.lookalikes: Clover (edible but lacks the sour taste). Shamrock plants (similar, also edible)

lookalikeDanger: Very low — heart-shaped leaflets and strong lemon taste are definitive
harvestNotes: Pick young leaves and flowers. Best in spring. Avoid harvesting in large quantities — high oxalic acid can irritate kidneys if eaten in excess.
nutritionHighlights: Vitamin C, oxalic acid (in moderation), antioxidants
preparationMethods: Raw in salads, as trail snack, garnish, lemonade-substitute tea, soup flavoring
medicinalUses: Vitamin C source, digestive stimulant, thirst quencher
ecologicalRole: Ground cover in forest ecosystems, nitrogen indicator
safetyWarnings: Avoid in large quantities — oxalic acid can affect kidney health
Avoid if you have kidney stones or gout

name: Lamb's Quarters
description: One of the most nutritious and abundant edible wild plants in North America. A common garden 'weed' that outperforms spinach in protein and calcium content. Young plants are particularly excellent cooked or raw.

identification.leaves: Diamond or goose-foot shaped with toothed margins; dusty whitish-gray coating (like powdered sugar) on new growth, especially undersides
identification.stem: Green with longitudinal stripes of green/white/pink; upright, branching
identification.flower: Tiny, green, in dense spike-like clusters at stem tips
identification.fruit: Tiny black seeds in papery husks — historically used as a grain
identification.lookalikes: Pigweed (edible — similar habitat, but lacks the whitish powdery coating). Nightshade (toxic — look different: dark berries, different leaf shape)

lookalikeDanger: Low — the powdery silvery coating on new growth is very distinctive
harvestNotes: Harvest young top 15–20cm of plant before flowering. Wash well to remove silvery coating. Can be harvested repeatedly by cutting — will regrow.
nutritionHighlights: Protein, calcium (higher than milk by weight), iron, vitamins A, C, K, B2
preparationMethods: Cooked like spinach, stir-fries, soups, smoothies (young leaves raw), seeds ground as flour
medicinalUses: Iron deficiency support, vitamin C source, anti-inflammatory
ecologicalRole: Soil nitrogen indicator, bird seed source, fast colonizer of disturbed ground
safetyWarnings: Contains oxalates — cook for regular consumption
Avoid near roadsides with heavy traffic/pesticide spray
Contains saponins in seeds — rinse seeds well before use

name: Chicken of the Woods
habitat: On dead or living hardwood trees
description: A spectacular bracket fungus that grows in vivid orange-yellow shelving clusters on trees. Has a mild, meaty, chicken-like taste and texture. One of the safest mushrooms for beginners because it has no dangerous lookalikes.

identification.stem: Stalkless, grows in overlapping shelf-like brackets directly from wood
identification.fruit: Bright orange upper surface, pale yellow-white pore surface underneath (no gills — tiny pores); rubbery-tough when old, tender when young
identification.lookalikes: Hen of the Woods / Maitake (Grifola frondosa) — similar habitat but brown/gray overlapping fronds from the base, also edible and excellent

lookalikeDanger: Very low — no toxic lookalikes in North America. The vivid orange-yellow color is unmistakable. Note: avoid specimens growing on yew, eucalyptus, or black locust — can cause GI upset in some people.
harvestNotes: Harvest young, tender outer edges only — avoid old, tough, white chalky portions. Cut with knife, leaving some behind for regrowth. Check pore surface — should be white/yellow (not brown = old).
nutritionHighlights: Protein, fiber, B vitamins, antioxidants, ergosterol (Vitamin D precursor)
preparationMethods: Sautéed, fried as chicken substitute, tacos, soups, dried
ecologicalRole: Important decomposer — breaks down dead hardwood, recycling nutrients into the forest
safetyWarnings: Cook thoroughly — raw can cause GI upset
Avoid from yew (Taxus) or eucalyptus trees
Some people with sulfite sensitivity may react — start with small amount
Avoid old, dry, chalky specimens

name: Purslane
description: A prostrate, fleshy-leaved succulent that is one of the world's most widely eaten wild plants. Contains the highest omega-3 fatty acid content of any leafy plant. A hot-weather garden 'weed' that is far more valuable than most planted crops.

identification.leaves: Thick, succulent, spatula-shaped, glossy green; paddle-like
identification.stem: Reddish-brown, fleshy, prostrate; thick and succulent
identification.flower: Tiny, 5-petaled, yellow, open only in morning sunshine
identification.lookalikes: Spurges (toxic — look for milky sap). Hairy-stemmed purslane (edible). Always confirm fleshy leaves, reddish stems, and NO milky sap.

lookalikeDanger: Low-moderate — spurges have similar prostrate habit but produce white milky sap when broken. Purslane has clear/mucilaginous (not milky) sap.
harvestNotes: Harvest tender stem tips and leaves. Best before flowering. Rinse well — often grows in gritty soil. Excellent in hot months when many other greens have bolted.
nutritionHighlights: Omega-3 fatty acids (highest of any vegetable), vitamins A, C, E, magnesium, potassium
preparationMethods: Raw in salads, lightly sautéed, pickled, in soups, yogurt with herbs
safetyWarnings: Contains oxalic acid — avoid in very large quantities
Confirm no milky sap (indicates toxic spurge lookalike)

name: Pawpaw
description: America's largest native fruit — a tropical-tasting custard fruit with notes of mango, banana, and vanilla. Grows as a small, understory tree in rich bottomland forests. Increasingly rare due to habitat loss but beginning to be cultivated.

identification.leaves: Large (20–30cm), obovate (wider at tip), alternate, drooping; tropical appearance; strong unpleasant smell when crushed
identification.stem: Small tree or large shrub; smooth gray bark with small wart-like lenticels; forms colony clumps via root suckers
identification.flower: Deep maroon/purple, 3 outer petals, about 3cm wide; appear before leaves in early spring; slight fetid smell
identification.fruit: Green to yellowish-green, mango/kidney-shaped, 5–15cm long; soft, custard-like, cream-to-yellow flesh; 2 rows of large brown seeds
identification.lookalikes: No dangerous lookalikes in North America — very distinctive large leaves and unique fruit

harvestNotes: Fruit is ripe when soft (like a ripe mango) and falls from tree at a touch. Does not store or ship well — eat within 2–3 days. Do NOT eat unripe green fruit — can cause nausea.
nutritionHighlights: Vitamin C, potassium, iron, amino acids, antioxidants; more protein than most temperate fruits
preparationMethods: Eat fresh, ice cream, smoothies, bread (like banana bread), frozen custard
medicinalUses: Bark has been researched for cancer-fighting acetogenins (Annonaceae)
ecologicalRole: Host plant for zebra swallowtail butterfly (sole larval host). Wildlife food source. Riparian habitat plant.
safetyWarnings: Eat only fully ripe, soft fruit
Seeds and skin are mildly toxic — remove before eating
Some people experience skin irritation from handling the fruit — wear gloves if sensitive

name: Common Chickweed
description: A cool-season annual that carpets garden beds and lawns in winter and early spring when little else is growing. The tender, mild-flavored greens are one of the best winter forage plants in temperate climates. A valuable fresh green when little else is available.

identification.leaves: Small, oval to egg-shaped, bright green, opposite pairs on stem; slightly pointed tip
identification.stem: Thin, weak, sprawling; key ID feature: single line of fine hairs running along one side of stem (alternates at each leaf pair)
identification.flower: Tiny white, 5 deeply notched petals (looks like 10 petals); about 5mm wide
identification.lookalikes: Scarlet pimpernel (toxic — orange/pink flowers, no hairs on stem). Spurge (toxic — milky sap). Always look for the single row of hairs on the stem.

lookalikeDanger: Low-moderate — scarlet pimpernel can grow in similar areas. Confirm the single hair-line on the stem and white notched flowers.
harvestNotes: Harvest entire mats of young growth — snip with scissors. Rinse well. Best before flowering. Avoid yellowing or slimy patches. One of the best winter-to-spring salad greens.
nutritionHighlights: Vitamin C, calcium, iron, magnesium, rutin
preparationMethods: Raw in salads, cooked like spinach (briefly), pesto, sandwiches
medicinalUses: Anti-inflammatory (topical for itching), gentle laxative, vitamin C source
ecologicalRole: Early-season soil cover preventing erosion, early insect food source
safetyWarnings: Avoid plants with milky sap (spurge lookalike)
Wash thoroughly — often grows in bird-frequented areas
